summary: Jeff Atwood expresses frustration with WYSIWYG editors that hide formatting tags from users, causing unexpected document corruption when users accidentally delete invisible markup. He argues that editors should at minimum provide an option to reveal formatting tags, comparing document editing in Word to 'walking through a field of formatting land mines.' He notes that browser-based editors like FreeTextBox at least allow switching to HTML view. Ultimately, he declares a preference for plain text editing with visible markup over WYSIWYG approaches.
key_points: Invisible formatting tags cause unpredictable document corruption when accidentally deleted
WYSIWYG editors should provide an option to reveal hidden formatting codes
Microsoft Word is particularly problematic, with innocuous edits destroying document formatting
Browser-based WYSIWYG editors are preferable because they allow switching to HTML view
Plain text editing with visible markup is more reliable than hidden formatting systems
money_quotes: The problem with invisible formatting tags is that they're, well, invisible.
Editing a Word document is a nerve wracking experience not unlike walking through a field of formatting land mines.
I like my markup clean, simple, and most of all – visible.
Thank goodness for my old friend, CTRL+Z.
Give us a way to optionally reveal the formatting tags in the document so I can tell what the heck I'm deleting.
key_insight: Document editing systems that hide their underlying structure from users create fragile, unpredictable experiences where users cannot understand or control the effects of their edits.
